comments:
Summary:
This paper investigates whether syllable-aware neural language models can outperform character-aware models (notably Char-CNN). The authors implement several syllable-based word embedding strategies (recurrent, convolutional, additive, concatenation, attention-weighted averages), run a two-stage model selection and hyperparameter tuning procedure on PTB under controlled parameter budgets, and evaluate the best models across multiple languages and dataset sizes. Main findings are that syllable-aware models do not beat competitive character-aware models in perplexity, although the best syllable model achieves comparable performance with substantially fewer parameters and faster training. A brief comparison with morpheme-based segmentation is included and source code is released.

Strengths:
- Thorough empirical exploration of multiple syllable-aware architectures and sensible comparison protocol (parameter-budget matching, two-stage tuning).
- Experiments across multiple data sizes and languages strengthen the empirical claim about scalability differences.
- Useful analyses (error overlap, PPL by token frequency, PCA of embeddings, testing RHN replacement) that help explain why Char-CNN outperforms syllable models.
- Code release increases reproducibility.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- The syllabification relies on Liang’s hyphenation algorithm (grapheme-based), which can be noisy and language-dependent; this limitation is acknowledged but deserves more analysis. The performance gap may partly stem from imperfect syllabification rather than an intrinsic inferiority of syllable representations.
- Some experimental details are incomplete or deferred: which six languages were used and precise dataset sizes for DATA-S / DATA-L are only partially described in the provided text. Syl-CNN results on the larger dataset are missing due to compute limits, which weakens cross-model comparisons at scale.
- The morpheme experiment is promising but under-explored: tuning was limited and results are preliminary; more thorough tuning could change conclusions.
- All evaluation is focused on word-level perplexity. It would be useful to see whether downstream tasks (e.g., tagging, NER, or speech-related tasks where syllables matter) benefit from syllable-aware inputs.
- No statistical significance testing is reported for PPL differences.

Suggestions for authors:
- Provide more analysis of the syllabification quality (error rates, examples) and, if possible, compare with phoneme-based or gold-standard syllabifications for at least one language.
- Report complete experimental details: the list of languages, exact dataset sizes, full hyperparameter settings of the reported models, and wall-clock training times for all configurations (some are in appendices but ensure completeness).
- If compute permits, run the missing Syl-CNN experiments on DATA-L or explicitly justify why results on DATA-S suffice.
- Expand the morpheme-based study or clearly mark it as exploratory future work.
- Consider including downstream-task evaluations or transfer experiments to better motivate the practical utility of syllable-aware representations.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid empirical study that addresses a reasonable hypothesis (are syllables a useful intermediate unit between characters and morphemes for NLM?). The negative result is reported carefully, with thoughtful analyses and practical findings (fewer parameters, faster training). The work will be of interest to the subword-representation and language-modeling community, though its impact could be increased by deeper treatment of syllabification quality and broader evaluation. I recommend acceptance at a venue that values empirical studies and negative results (workshop or poster track).
